system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 12:48 AM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/CLAUDE.md]
# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

{"output": "# Law Firm Wiki — Compilation Log

## 2026-04-12 — Initial Compilation

**Compiler**: Wiki Compiler Agent
**Cases Processed**: 3
**Source Data**:
- Case A (negotiation phase): 42 activity logs — transfer case from prior counsel, Indiana MVA, incarcerated client, pre-settlement funding, treatment gap, single provider
- Case B (treatment phase): 76 activity logs — new intake, treatment to demand transition, PIP management (Erie), BI claim (Progressive), multiple providers, engaged family contact
- Case C (negotiation phase): 225 activity logs — full lifecycle from intake through negotiation, PIP (State Farm), BI (State Farm), health insurance lien (Anthem), lien disputes, collections issues, SOL countdown

**Articles Created**: 12 concept articles, 3 connection articles
**Confidence Level**: All articles at "low" (1-3 cases each) — will improve as more cases are compiled

### Concepts Created
1. `intake-and-onboarding-workflow` — Standard intake workflow from initial contact through file setup
2. `file-transfer-from-prior-counsel` — Process for obtaining files from prior law firms
3. `treatment-gap-defense` — Adjuster tactic of citing treatment gaps to reduce value
4. `pip-claims-management` — PIP bill submission, tracking, and follow-up
5. `medical-records-collection` — Records/bills request process from providers
6. `client-communication-through-family` — Patterns when family members are primary contacts
7. `demand-sent-by-prior-firm` — Edge case when prior firm already sent a demand
8. `lien-management-health-insurance` — Health insurance subrogation lien audit and dispute
9. `pre-settlement-funding-obligations` — Lawsuit loans and their impact on net recovery
10. `case-phase-transitions` — Phase lifecycle and transition prerequisites
11. `attorney-review-triggers` — Events requiring attorney involvement
12. `client-expectations-management` — Managing client anxiety and timeline expectations
13. `medical-bill-collections-anxiety` — Bills going to collections during case
14. `statute-of-limitations-monitoring` — SOL countdown and escalation

### Connections Created
1. `treatment-gaps-affect-settlement-value` — How treatment gaps + funding obligations create low-value outcomes
2. `pip-delays-cause-collections-anxiety` — How PIP delays cascade into collections and client pressure
3. `records-completion-gates-demand` — How records delays bottleneck the entire case timeline

### Key Patterns Identified
- **PIP is the biggest operational bottleneck**: Bills submitted to PIP can sit unprocessed for 60+ days, causing downstream collection issues
- **Treatment gap is the most common adjuster defense**: Observed in multiple cases, directly tied to lower offers
- **Client communication through family is the norm, not the exception**: 2 of 3 cases had a family member as primary contact
- **Records collection delays cascade**: Every week of delay in records extends the overall case timeline
- **Attorney involvement is episodic, not continuous**: The attorney checks in periodically and makes directional decisions, but daily work is paralegal-driven
- **Phase transitions are often delayed**: Cases can sit in a phase for weeks/months past when they should have transitioned

### Gaps Identified
- No settlement disbursement examples
- No demand letter construction examples
- No negotiation counter-offer exchanges
- No litigation workflow
- Limited data on provider billing negotiations
- No UIM (Underinsured Motorist) claim examples despite one case having a UIM claim

## 2026-04-12 — Compilation: robin-wilder-hamilton (Case D)

**Compiler**: Wiki Compiler Agent
**Cases Processed**: 1 (cumulative: 4)
**Source Data**:
- Case D (negotiation phase): 760 activity logs — KY auto accident, veteran client treated through VA Medical Center, VA lien managed by OGC Revenue Law Group, PIP (Auto Owners), BI (Sea Harbor Insurance), multiple adjuster reassignments, 2.5+ year case lifecycle, client communication breakdown

**Articles Created**: 4 concept articles, 1 connection article
**Articles Updated**: 7 existing concept articles
**Confidence Level**: All new articles at "low" (single case); updated articles remain "low" (2-4 cases each)

### Concepts Created
1. `va-lien-management` — VA lien lifecycle under Federal Medical Care Recovery Act; OGC Revenue Law Group process; GCL case numbers; PIP payment of VA liens
2. `government-entity-records-collection` — How VA/government records collection differs from private providers; 12-18 month timelines; multiple fax lines; MyHealtheVet portal
3. `adjuster-reassignment-patterns` — Multiple adjuster changes on single claim; "closer" adjuster pattern; fresh-eyes opportunity; communication impacts
4. `multi-carrier-negotiation` — Coordinating BI + PIP + VA lien simultaneously; "new money" vs total value; bill routing matrix; settlement math

### Connection Created
1. `va-liens-complicate-demand-timeline` — How VA involvement creates cascading delays: slow records → incomplete lien data → blocked demand → stalled negotiation → client frustration

### Concepts Updated
1. `lien-management-health-insurance` — Added VA cross-reference and government vs. private lien distinction
2. `medical-records-collection` — Added VA records evidence (12+ month timeline, incomplete records, client self-report gap)
3. `client-expectations-management` — Added extreme case: repeated unanswered voicemails over 2 months, client saying "I just want everything settled... with whatever"

### Key Patterns Identified
- **VA liens are fundamentally different from private health insurance liens**: Federal legal authority, OGC Revenue Law Group as counterpart, GCL case numbers, months-long timelines
- **VA records collection creates the worst bottleneck observed**: 12-18 months from request to receipt; records arrive incomplete; client must supplement
- **PIP can pay VA liens directly**: A useful strategy to resolve the VA lien early using PIP benefits
- **Adjuster reassignment can be an opportunity**: New "closer" adjusters are proactive and motivated to settle
- **Unrelated treatment attribution is a variant of the treatment gap defense**: Adjuster does not deny treatment but attributes it to a different injury
- **Client communication failures escalate dramatically in long cases**: 2+ year cases with VA delays produce the most extreme client frustration
- **Circular dependency trap**: VA wants treatment dates to reopen case; firm needs records to identify dates — break with client MyHealtheVet access
- **Attorney must eventually make the call to proceed with imperfect information**: Waiting for perfect VA records can extend cases indefinitely

### Gaps Identified
- VA lien negotiation/reduction: unknown whether VA liens can be negotiated down like private liens
- Medicare/Medicaid conditional payment liens: no cases observed
- VA CHAMPVA billing: referenced in VA correspondence but not observed in case data
- Long-case client retention: how to prevent clients from accepting any offer out of frustration

### Connections Created
1. `premises-liability-requires-litigation-prep` — PL cases should prepare for litigation from day one (certified records, expert budgeting, evidence preservation)
2. `client-frustration-drives-premature-settlement` — How poor communication leads to premature settlement or client attrition

### Articles Updated
1. `case-phase-transitions` — Added evidence of backward phase transition (Settlement -> Negotiation) and PL cases skipping demand phase; evidence_count 3->5
2. `medical-records-collection` — Added evidence of certified records for litigation, provider discovery during records review; confidence low->medium, evidence_count 3->5

### Key Patterns Identified from New Cases
- **Premises liability cases are litigation-bound**: PL cases against commercial property owners with dedicated defense counsel are much more likely to proceed to full litigation than auto accident cases
- **Expert work adds months to the timeline**: FCE, IME, and vocational reports require significant scheduling coordination, and rescheduling by clients or providers is common
- **Records review reveals hidden providers**: During demand preparation, reviewing records from one provider often reveals referrals to previously unknown providers, delaying the demand
- **Premature "Settlement" labeling causes client confusion**: When a case is labeled "Settlement" before the client agrees, staff confusion and client frustration result
- **Client frustration follows a predictable 4-stage escalation**: Polite inquiry -> urgent demand -> threats -> repeated unanswered attempts
- **Backward phase transitions occur**: Cases can move from Settlement back to Negotiation when clients reject offers
- **Adjusters proactively check on cases near SOL**: BI adjusters may call to follow up on demand status, especially as SOL approaches
- **Litigation requires certified records**: Standard copies of medical records are insufficient for court use; must be re-requested as certified if not done initially

### Gaps Partially Addressed

- **Litigation workflow**: Now documented as `litigation-process-patterns` from one PL case (need auto accident litigation examples for comparison)
- **Negotiation counter-offers**: `negotiation-after-demand` captures the offer-rejection pattern but still lacks documented counter-offer exchanges

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Premises Liability Case Patterns
    19|
    20|> Premises liability (PL) cases — typically slip-and-fall incidents at commercial properties — follow a different trajectory than auto accident cases. They are more likely to proceed to litigation, involve property owner defense counsel early, and require more extensive expert work (FCE, IME, vocational/economic experts). The liability analysis focuses on property owner notice and maintenance rather than traffic fault.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|PL cases differ from auto accident cases in several key ways:
    25|
    26|### Liability Analysis
    27|- **Property owner notice**: Was the hazardous condition known to the owner? How long did it exist?
    28|- **Maintenance records**: Were inspection and maintenance protocols followed?
    29|- **Incident reports**: Was an incident report filed at the time of the fall?
    30|- **Surveillance footage**: Commercial properties may have video of the incident; preservation requests should be sent immediately
    31|- **Third-party defendants**: Banks, stores, and other commercial entities typically have dedicated defense counsel (not insurance adjusters)
    32|
    33|### Litigation Tendency
    34|- PL cases are more likely to proceed to litigation than auto accident cases
    35|- Defense counsel for commercial property owners may aggressively litigate through discovery, depositions, and motions practice
    36|- Mediation is commonly attempted before trial
    37|
    38|### Expert Work
    39|- **Functional Capacity Evaluations (FCE)**: Used to document physical limitations from the injury
    40|- **Independent Medical Examinations (IME)**: May be requested by either side; amendments may be needed as treatment progresses
    41|- **Economist/Vocational Reports**: When the injury affects earning capacity, vocational economics experts are retained
    42|
    43|### Timeline
    44|- PL cases tend to run significantly longer than auto accident cases (3-6+ years observed vs. 1-2 years for typical MVA)
    45|- Discovery deadlines, motions practice, and mediation scheduling extend the timeline
    46|
    47|## Evidence
    48|
    49|- In a premises liability case (slip-and-fall at a commercial bank, 2019 incident), the case progressed through litigation with complaint filing, interrogatories (IROG), discovery, depositions, mediation scheduling, IME, FCE, and economist report requests — spanning 2019-2025+. Defense counsel for the property owner actively participated in discovery and motions practice. Mediation was scheduled then cancelled. The case required certified medical records for litigation (not just demand copies).
    50|- The same case involved multiple medical providers across orthopedics, neurosurgery, PCP, and imaging facilities, with the attorney directing additional imaging (MRI) based on IME findings.
    51|- Tax documentation was required for economic damages claims, and the defense counsel specifically challenged tax document accuracy (wrong year provided).
    52|- In a second PL case (fall at commercial property, DOI 9/2023), the case was transferred from prior counsel with SOL approaching within 60 days. The firm onboarded and moved to litigation (complaint filing + discovery requests) the same day as file setup — skipping demand entirely. Multiple defendants and their employer/business entities were named, each represented by separate defense counsel. The case involved 15+ medical providers, insurance carrier service via out-of-state sheriff, and multi-law-firm defense representation. The client exhibited a pattern of frequent missed calls (20+ over 7 months) with periodic attorney return calls.
    53|
    54|## Agent Guidance
    55|
    56|1. At intake for PL cases, immediately send a preservation letter to the property owner requesting incident reports, maintenance logs, and surveillance footage
    57|2. Identify the property owner's insurance carrier AND their defense counsel — PL cases often have dedicated law firms representing the property owner
    58|3. Plan for litigation from the start: request certified records (not just copies) from medical providers
    59|4. Budget for expert expenses early: FCE ($500-600+), IME, vocational/economic reports
    60|5. Track discovery deadlines meticulously — interrogatories, document production, deposition scheduling
    61|6. Coordinate mediation scheduling between all parties (mediator, defense counsel, client) well in advance
    62|7. When the attorney directs additional medical evaluation based on IME/FCE findings, prioritize scheduling
    63|
    64|## Pitfalls
    65|
    66|- Treating a PL case like an auto accident case (PL is more complex and litigation-heavy)
    67|- Failing to request surveillance footage promptly — it may be overwritten
    68|- Not requesting certified records for litigation (regular copies may be insufficient for court)
    69|- Providing incorrect documents to defense counsel (e.g., wrong tax year) — verify all documents before production
    70|- Not coordinating expert evaluations (FCE, IME) early enough — scheduling can take weeks/months
    71|- Allowing mediation to be cancelled without promptly rescheduling
    72|
    73|## Related
    74|
    75|- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — PL cases frequently enter litigation
    76|- [[medical-records-collection]] — PL cases require certified records for litigation
    77|- [[expert-reports-and-evaluations]] — FCE, IME, and vocational reports are common in PL
    78|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — PL cases may skip demand phase and go directly to litigation
    79|", "total_lines": 78, "file_size": 5713, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Workers' Compensation Case Patterns
     3|slug: workers-compensation-case-patterns
     4|phase: [phase_0_onboarding, phase_1_file_setup, phase_2_treatment, phase_7_litigation]
     5|category: edge-cases
     6|tags: [workers-comp, wc, dwc, brc, ime, form-106, adjuster, defense-counsel]
     7|confidence: low
     8|evidence_count: 1
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - case-phase-transitions
    13|  - expert-reports-and-evaluations
    14|  - litigation-process-patterns
    15|  - medical-records-collection
    16|---
    17|
    18|# Workers' Compensation Case Patterns
    19|
    20|> Workers' compensation cases differ fundamentally from personal injury cases in their procedural framework, involved parties, and resolution mechanisms. WC cases are governed by state Department of Workers' Compensation (DWC) rules rather than civil court procedures, involve employer's WC insurance carriers rather than at-fault BI carriers, and resolve through administrative proceedings (Benefit Review Conferences) rather than jury trials.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Key Differences from PI Cases
    25|1. **Intake**: CaseOpp system may use the wrong contract type (PI vs WC) — must verify and re-send WC-specific intake documents
    26|2. **Insurance Setup**: LOR sent to WC adjuster (not BI/PIP). DWC insurance lookup required to identify the WC carrier
    27|3. **Treatment Monitoring**: Extended treatment periods common (2+ years). Client may undergo surgery, PT, injections, and still be in active treatment. Treatment includes hardware placement (plates, screws)
    28|4. **Administrative Proceedings**: BRC (Benefit Review Conference) hearings are telephonic, scheduled by DWC, with assigned passcodes. BRCs are frequently rescheduled
    29|5. **Forms**: DWC Form 106 required for various proceedings — sent to client via DocuSign for execution per defense counsel request
    30|6. **Defense Counsel**: Employer/carrier represented by defense firm that exchanges correspondence about Form 106, IME scheduling, and case status
    31|7. **IME**: Scheduled through Commonwealth IME (same service as PI), but invoiced and tracked through WC process. IME is paid by the firm
    32|
    33|### WC Case Lifecycle
    34|- Intake & File Setup (WC-specific LOR, DWC carrier lookup)
    35|- Active Treatment (potentially years — surgery, PT, injections, MMI assessment)
    36|- DWC Proceedings (BRC hearings, rescheduling)
    37|- IME / Medical Evaluation
    38|- Defense Counsel Negotiations
    39|- Resolution (settlement or hearing)
    40|
    41|## Evidence
    42|
    43|- In Case F (WC case, DOI 12/2022), client was injured at a commercial restaurant. The case involved:
    44|  - Intake through CaseOpp with wrong contract type (PI instead of WC), requiring re-send of WC intake docs
    45|  - WC carrier identified via DWC lookup (Accident Fund Insurance)
    46|  - LOR sent to WC adjuster via fax on day 1
    47|  - Extensive treatment: ER visit, orthopedic surgery (plate + 10 screws in wrist), physical therapy, shoulder injections, pain management, MRI, dry needling — spanning 2.5+ years
    48|  - DWC Form 106 sent to client via DocuSign multiple times per defense counsel requests
    49|  - BRC hearings scheduled telephonically, rescheduled multiple times (rescheduled from Feb to Mar 2025)
    50|  - Defense counsel (Pohl & Aubrey) actively exchanged correspondence about IME scheduling and Form 106
    51|  - IME scheduled through Commonwealth IME — coordination via email, invoice tracked
    52|  - WC adjuster communications via email about claim status
    53|  - Case timeline: 3+ years from DOI and still active (Jan 2023 to Mar 2026+)
    54|
    55|## Agent Guidance
    56|
    57|1. Verify the contract type is WC (not PI) during intake — CaseOpp may default to PI
    58|2. Use DWC insurance lookup to identify the WC carrier
    59|3. Send WC-specific LOR (not PI LOR) to the WC adjuster
    60|4. Track DWC Form 106 requests and ensure client executes via DocuSign promptly
    61|5. Monitor BRC scheduling and rescheduling — update all parties on new dates
    62|6. WC treatment timelines are often much longer than PI — set client expectations accordingly
    63|7. Track IME scheduling and invoicing through the WC process
    64|8. Communicate with defense counsel about Form 106 and IME scheduling per DWC rules
    65|9. Bill review and submission goes to WC carrier, not PIP
    66|
    67|## Pitfalls
    68|
    69|- Using PI intake documents instead of WC-specific forms
    70|- Not performing DWC insurance lookup (WC carriers differ from auto/premises carriers)
    71|- Applying PI case timeline expectations to WC cases (WC cases regularly span 3+ years)
    72|- Missing BRC rescheduling notices (these are time-sensitive)
    73|- Not tracking Form 106 execution status
    74|- Confusing WC process milestones with PI phase transitions
    75|
    76|## Related

    17|---
    18|
    19|# Litigation Process Patterns
    20|
    21|> When PI cases proceed to litigation, they enter a structured legal process involving complaint filing, discovery (interrogatories, document production), depositions, motions practice, and potential mediation or trial. Litigation dramatically increases case complexity, timeline, and administrative burden on the paralegal team.
    22|
    23|## Pattern
    24|
    25|The litigation lifecycle follows a predictable sequence:
    26|
    27|### 1. Complaint Filing
    28|- Draft complaint with interrogatories (IROG)
    29|- File with the circuit court
    30|- Check service of process on defendant
    31|- Court assigns case number and division
    32|
    33|### 2. Discovery Phase
    34|- **Interrogatories**: Written questions exchanged between parties
    35|- **Supplemental responses**: As new information becomes available, supplemental discovery responses must be filed
    36|- **Document production**: Medical records, tax returns, employment records
    37|- **Subpoenas**: Third-party records (e.g., bank records from Hometown Bank) obtained via subpoena
    38|- **Timeline**: Court sets discovery deadlines; joint motions to extend are common
    39|
    40|### 3. Motions Practice
    41|- Motions to compel (when opposing party doesn't respond to discovery)
    42|- Motions to withdraw counsel
    43|- Joint motions for deadline extensions
    44|- Motion hearings may be remote or rescheduled
    45|
    46|### 4. Mediation
    47|- Typically scheduled after discovery is substantially complete
    48|- Mediator selected through national organizations (e.g., NADN)
    49|- Both parties must agree on mediator and date
    50|- Half-day sessions are common; can be via Zoom
    51|- Mediation may be cancelled and rescheduled
    52|
    53|### 5. Expert Coordination
    54|- FCE and IME reports ordered during litigation
    55|- Expert invoices must be tracked and paid
    56|- Reports may need amendments as treatment progresses
    57|
    58|### Court Filing System
    59|- Kentucky courts use eFiling with automated NEF (Notice of Electronic Filing) and NCP notifications
    60|- Multiple attorneys and paralegals receive notifications simultaneously
    61|
    62|## Evidence
    63|
    64|- In a premises liability case in litigation (Knox Circuit Court), the litigation spanned 3+ years (2022-2025+). Activities included: complaint filing with IROG, checking service, receiving motions, joint deadline extension motions, mediation scheduling and cancellation, IME amendment requests, FCE scheduling and rescheduling, economist report ordering, deposition scheduling, subpoena responses, and supplemental discovery responses.
    65|- Motion hearings were rescheduled multiple times (from 1/20/22 to 2/14/22 via Facebook notice from clerk).
    66|- Certified records were required for litigation — regular copies insufficient. Certification forms had to be corrected (date range "04/2014 thru Current").
    67|- Mediation was scheduled through the National Academy of Distinguished Neutrals (NADN) with a retired judge mediator, coordinated with defense counsel schedules.
    68|- In Case G (MVA, Boone County), complaint filed ~1 year post-accident. Full litigation lifecycle including discovery exchanges with defense counsel (Richardson Law Group), deposition scheduling via Kentuckiana Reporters, and mediation via Zoom. Mediation failed — defense offered "last best & final" amounts that were inadequate. Zoom mediation required coordination with clients on technical setup (muting, call-in numbers). The case remained active post-mediation failure.
    69|- In Case H (MVA, Barren County), complaint filed in Barren Circuit Court (23-CI-00659). The defendant's insurer (Kentucky Farm Bureau) filed a parallel subrogation action in District Court. Depositions were scheduled via Kentuckiana Reporters. Mediation was being scheduled via the same service. Service of process required checking via alias — "Check for Service - Issue Alias is not perfected" — indicating the defendant was difficult to serve.
    70|- In Case I (MVA, Jefferson Circuit Court), complaint filed ~8 months post-accident (24-CI-005774) due to disputed liability (BI only accepting 25%). Discovery followed, with defense counsel reviewing dash cam video. Depositions were scheduled via Zoom for both the client and a witness (companion passenger). Client nearly missed deposition — required multiple urgent texts ("Cecil, you good?", "Cecil you ready?", "Cecil?") before responding. Deposition completed successfully. Defense counsel served interrogatories and additional discovery.
    71|- In Case F (WC case), litigation followed a different track — DWC (Department of Workers' Compensation) proceedings with BRC (Benefit Review Conferences) scheduled telephonically. Defense counsel (Pohl & Aubrey) exchanged correspondence about Form 106 requests and IME scheduling. This WC litigation model is distinct from civil circuit court litigation.
    72|- In Case F (auto accident, litigation), discovery involved interrogatories and requests for production exchanged between plaintiff and defendant. Defense counsel (Dilbeck & Myers) engaged actively in scheduling depositions. The defendant's deposition was scheduled via Zoom, with the plaintiff's deposition to follow immediately after. The firm communicated with the client via text and phone to prepare for deposition. Defense counsel's responses to production requests were tracked through email exchanges.
    73|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), a UIM complaint was filed separately against the client's own insurer (Allstate) in Fayette Circuit Court. The insurer filed an answer to the complaint. Discovery commenced with the plaintiff's first set of discovery. The UIM adjuster received a courtesy copy of the complaint. This demonstrates that UIM litigation follows a parallel track to BI claims.
    74|- In Case H (auto accident, litigation with stolen vehicle), the case (22-CI-002878) involved multiple defendants and defense counsel from different firms. Key litigation events included: defense counsel requesting and receiving a 60-day extension to answer the complaint while attempting to get the insurer to accept coverage; an Examination Under Oath (EUO) of the defendant regarding the stolen vehicle claim; deposition scheduling disputes where defense counsel was "adamant" about timing constraints; unilateral scheduling of depositions when counsel could not agree; and audio/video recording requests from the court. The case lasted 4+ years from DOI through ongoing litigation.
    75|- In a multi-defendant auto accident case in litigation (Jefferson Circuit Court, 21-CI-004985, DOI 1/2021), the litigation spanned 4+ years (2021-2025+). Activities included: complaint filing, interrogatory responses to defense counsel, deposition scheduling with defense counsel, State Farm motion to dismiss (AOC 280 Final Submission for Adjudication), certified medical records collection via ChartSwap, government open records requests for police body cam footage (via Axon/Evidence.com), multiple ORRs to LMPD, amended subpoenas (to Verizon and Metro PCS for phone records), Louisville Metro public records requests, forwarding evidence to defense counsel (Dinsmore, Smith & Hoskins), and locating witnesses (other vehicle passengers). This is the most complex litigation case observed, involving multiple defense counsel, government records, and electronic evidence. The 4+ year timeline from DOI to ongoing litigation confirms that multi-defendant cases with uninsured drivers dramatically extend case duration.
    76|
    77|## Agent Guidance
    78|
    79|1. Create a litigation calendar at filing with all deadlines from the court's scheduling order
    80|2. Track every court notification (NEF, NCP) and file appropriately
    81|3. Maintain a discovery checklist: what's been requested, what's been produced, what's outstanding
    82|4. For supplemental discovery responses, track what new information needs to be disclosed
    83|5. Coordinate expert scheduling well in advance — FCE and IME providers have limited availability
    84|6. When mediator sends a Zoom link or scheduling confirmation, immediately distribute to all parties
    85|7. Monitor motion hearing schedule — courts frequently reschedule
    86|8. Keep a running litigation expense tracker (expert fees, filing fees, mediator fees)
    87|
    88|## Pitfalls
    89|
    90|- Missing court deadlines (can result in sanctions or adverse rulings)
    91|- Not tracking supplemental discovery obligations
    92|- Allowing expert reports to become stale (e.g., new treatment occurred after IME)
    93|- Not verifying certified records meet litigation requirements
    94|- Failing to respond to mediator scheduling promptly (can delay case months)
    95|- Not updating the trial calendar when deadlines change
    96|
    97|## Related
    98|
    99|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — PL cases are the most common path to litigation
   100|- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — SOL is the hard deadline for filing a complaint
   101|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — all litigation decisions require attorney involvement
   102|- [[medical-records-collection]] — litigation requires certified records, not just copies
   103|", "total_lines": 102, "file_size": 9263, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Medical Records and Bills Collection
    19|
    20|> Collecting complete medical records and bills from all treatment providers is a prerequisite for demand preparation. This process involves sending HIPAA authorizations, formal record/bill requests via fax and email, and persistent follow-up when providers are slow to respond. Gaps in records collection directly delay case resolution.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The records collection workflow follows this sequence:
    25|
    26|1. **Provider Identification**: Confirm all treatment providers with the client, including ER, PCP, specialists, imaging
    27|2. **Request Preparation**: Generate HIPAA medical authorization forms and formal records/billing request letters for each provider
    28|3. **Request Delivery**: Send via fax (using RingCentral or similar) and/or email to the provider's records department
    29|4. **Response Tracking**: Track which providers have responded with records vs. bills vs. both
    30|5. **Follow-Up**: If no response within 2-3 weeks, follow up
    31|6. **Client Assistance**: Ask clients to help obtain records from their patient portal (MyChart) when formal requests are delayed
    32|7. **Completeness Check**: Verify all providers are accounted for before moving to demand
    33|
    34|## Evidence
    35|
    36|- In Case B, records/billing requests were sent to St. Elizabeth Florence Hospital via email to a specific contact (Dawn.Probst@stelizabeth.com). The billing info was returned same day. However, getting complete records from Christ Hospital Physicians Primary Care took significantly longer.
    37|- In Case C, records were requested from Baptist Health Louisville via fax on 4/29/2024. Records were received by 5/13/2024 (14 days). A task to "review records and add to Timeline" was created once received.
    38|- In Case B, the client's spouse provided medical visit details from the patient portal (MyChart) while waiting for formal records, which helped move the case forward.
    39|- In Case C, the attorney flagged that records collection had stalled with "no action" for a month, emphasizing that negotiation cannot proceed without complete records.
    40|- In Case D (VA patient), records requests to VA Medical Center Lexington took over 12 months to yield results. Initial request mailed Nov 2023; VA OGC responded Jul 2024. Even after receiving records, they were incomplete — the client reported additional MRIs, physical therapy (VA IPRP program), and injections not in the file. The firm had to ask the client to review records and identify what was missing. The attorney noted: "We're missing a lot of stuff, and that's just part and parcel to the VA."
    41|- In Case D (premises liability, litigation), records had to be requested as CERTIFIED copies for use in litigation — standard copies were insufficient. The certification required correction (date range "04/2014 thru Current"). Records were delivered via Box.com file sharing from ROI quality analysts (e.g., Medicopy). Multiple providers were involved: Grace Health (PCP), Norton/Khalily (orthopedics), CHI St. Joseph (orthopedics + radiology), Baptist Health Corbin, and Corbin Imaging. Records were re-requested to get updated records during active litigation.
    42|- In Case E (auto accident), records review during demand preparation revealed referrals to providers not previously on file (White House Clinics, King's Eye Care). These had to be added and their records requested, delaying demand preparation. Records from one provider (H2 Health) were requested by calling, faxing, and re-faxing over weeks.
    43|- In Case D (20+ providers across 4 states), initial records/bills requests were sent in Feb-Apr 2023, but many providers had not responded by mid-2024, requiring a complete second round of "Certified Medical Records & Billing Request" letters in Aug 2024 — nearly 18 months after the first round. Multiple channels were used simultaneously (email, fax via RingCentral, ChartSwap, EpicLink). Provider confusion arose when billing names didn't match clinical names (e.g., "Norton Leatherman" vs "Norton Neuro & Spine" returned "No Records" despite bills existing).
    44|- In Case F (WC case), records were requested from Medical Center of Scottsville and Riverside Orthopedics via fax within the first week of file setup. The Med Center required a DocuSign-verified authorization (the standard fax copy was rejected), adding a round-trip delay. The provider also needed clarification on scope ("only ER records or all records"). Records arrived piecemeal — radiology CD first, then billing statements separately. A third-party billing entity (TeamHealth) sent bills that were associated with the hospital visit but under a different entity name. Multiple new providers were added over 2+ years as treatment progressed (pain management, shoulder injections, MRI facilities).
    45|- In Case H (MVA, air evac), records collection involved an air ambulance service (Air Evac EMS) requiring fax to a distant location. ChartSwap was used for TriStar Skyline Medical Center records retrieval. The case involved records from providers across multiple states (TN hospital where air-lifted, KY local providers). The air evacuation bill alone was $72K+, making complete billing documentation critical.
    46|- In Case G (MVA, companion cases), records collection was complicated by a companion case (daughter) requiring separate hospital records from Children's Hospital. The client was asked to obtain companion records but failed to do so over months (Oct 2024 to Feb 2025+). Chiropractic records from Southgate Chiropractic were also needed.
    47|- In Case I (MVA), records were requested from UofL Health St. Mary & Elizabeth Hospital and chiropractic provider via fax within the first week. Provider sent records via ChartSwap platform. The PIP adjuster proactively asked about medical records even before the firm's formal requests were complete.
    48|
    49|## Agent Guidance
    50|
    51|1. Create a provider checklist during file setup with records requested/received dates for each
    52|2. Send HIPAA authorizations and record requests within the first week of file setup
    53|3. Use both fax and email when possible — some providers respond faster to one channel
    54|4. Set follow-up tasks at 14-day intervals for each outstanding request
    55|5. Encourage clients to share records from patient portals as an interim measure
    56|6. Track records and bills separately — receiving one does not mean you have the other
    57|7. Do not move to demand preparation until ALL providers have been accounted for
    58|8. When the attorney asks about case status, have the records collection status ready
    59|
    60|## Pitfalls
    61|
    62|- Not confirming all providers with the client early (discovering a provider late delays everything)
    63|- Sending requests and not tracking responses
    64|- Not distinguishing between records received and bills received (you need both)
    65|- Letting the client's medical portal records substitute for formal provider records in the demand
    66|- Waiting too long to follow up on non-responsive providers
    67|- Requesting records under a billing entity name that differs from the clinical entity name (causes "no records found" responses)
    68|- Not anticipating that multi-state providers require different request methods and response timelines
    69|
    70|## Related
    71|
    72|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP needs bills to process; records collection feeds PIP workflow
    73|- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — demand cannot be sent until records are complete
    74|- [[provider-communication-patterns]] — how different providers respond to requests
    75|- [[government-entity-records-collection]] — VA and other government providers require distinct handling with much longer timelines
    76|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — PL cases in litigation require certified records
    77|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — records completion gates the demand phase
    78|", "total_lines": 77, "file_size": 8133, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Companion Case Management
    18|
    19|> When multiple family members are injured in the same accident, each client requires a separate case file but the cases must be coordinated. Companion cases share the same defendant, insurance carriers, and litigation timeline, but each plaintiff has independent medical treatment, damages, and settlement negotiations. The primary client (typically the parent/driver) often serves as the coordinator for all cases.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|1. **Intake**: One family member signs up first; firm discovers additional family members during the welcome call or initial contact
    24|2. **File Creation**: Each companion gets a separate case file with separate intake documents
    25|3. **Coordination**: Parent/primary client is expected to facilitate the companion's document signing, provide their information (SSN, medical providers, insurance)
    26|4. **Records Collection**: Each companion's medical providers must be identified and records requested independently — this often falls on the primary client to facilitate
    27|5. **Litigation**: Companions may be joined in one lawsuit (e.g., "Plaintiff A et al. vs. Defendant") or filed separately. Depositions are scheduled sequentially for companions
    28|6. **Settlement**: Each companion's settlement is evaluated independently, but mediation may address all cases simultaneously
    29|
    30|## Evidence
    31|
    32|- In Case G (MVA), the client's daughter was identified as a companion case during the welcome call. Her Filevine file had not been created yet, requiring coordination. The primary client was asked repeatedly over 6+ months to obtain Children's Hospital records for the companion but failed to do so. Both cases were joined in one lawsuit (Boone 24-CI-00452). During mediation via Zoom, both cases received separate LBF (last best & final) offers. Mediation failed for both.
    33|- In Case I (MVA), a companion witness (Dana Jackson) was deposed alongside the client. Depositions were scheduled sequentially via Zoom — the client was told "we are going to do Dana's deposition first so just chill and I will shoot you a text when we are about done." Coordinating two depositions on the same day required active text communication to manage timing.
    34|
    35|## Agent Guidance
    36|
    37|1. During intake, ask whether other family members were in the vehicle and injured
    38|2. Create separate case files for each companion immediately — do not delay
    39|3. Send intake documents to companions as early as possible
    40|4. Track records collection for each companion independently
    41|5. When the primary client is the gateway to companion information, set explicit deadlines and follow up
    42|6. For litigation, coordinate with the attorney on whether to file jointly or separately
    43|7. Schedule depositions for companions on the same day when possible to minimize coordination burden
    44|8. During mediation, prepare separate settlement evaluations for each companion
    45|
    46|## Pitfalls
    47|
    48|- Discovering companion cases days/weeks after the primary intake (records requests are delayed)
    49|- Relying on the primary client to obtain companion's information without explicit deadlines
    50|- Not creating a separate case file (tracking companion in notes on primary case)
    51|- Not requesting companion's medical records independently (assuming they're covered by the primary request)
    52|- Scheduling conflicts when companions need to attend the same deposition/mediation session
    53|
    54|## Related
    55|
    56|- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — companion cases are discovered during intake
    57|- [[client-communication-through-family]] — primary client acts as intermediary for companions
    58|- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — companion cases may be joined in one lawsuit
    59|", "total_lines": 58, "file_size": 4053, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Intake and Onboarding Workflow
    18|
    19|> The intake process converts a potential client into an active case. It involves collecting personal information, executing the retainer agreement, setting up insurance claims, identifying medical providers, and establishing communication channels. The workflow must be completed rapidly to preserve evidence and begin the claims process.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Standard intake workflow observed across cases:
    24|
    25|1. **Initial Contact**: Client (or family member) reaches out, usually by phone. Case manager gathers preliminary accident details, injuries, and insurance information.
    26|2. **Information Request**: Firm requests: Name, DOB, SSN, phone number, copy of health insurance card, names/addresses of hospital and PCP.
    27|3. **Contract Execution**: Intake documents sent via VineSign (electronic signature platform) for completion. Documents include fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, and other intake forms.
    28|4. **Welcome Call**: Once docs are signed, firm conducts a welcome call to confirm treatment providers and introduce the client to the process.
    29|5. **Phase Change**: Case moves from Lead/PNC to File Setup.
    30|6. **Insurance Setup**: Send Letters of Representation to both BI (at-fault) and PIP (client's own) carriers. Request acknowledgment.
    31|7. **Provider Setup**: Add all medical providers to the system. Send records/billing requests.
    32|8. **Phase Change**: Case moves from File Setup to Treatment.
    33|
    34|Timeline: This entire workflow typically spans 3-7 days from initial contact.
    35|
    36|## Evidence
    37|
    38|- In Case B, the initial contact came from the client's husband who provided detailed accident information, insurance claim numbers, and adjuster contacts. The firm requested personal info (DOB, SSN, etc.), sent VineSign intake docs, and received signed docs within 3 days.
    39|- In Case C, the intake and welcome call happened on day 1 (4/25/2024). Insurance claims were set up and LORs sent the same day. Records requests went out by 4/29.
    40|- In Case A, intake was complicated by the prior firm transfer — mom signed intake docs via VineSign, but the file transfer from the prior firm took over a week.
    41|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA), the BI adjuster proactively called the firm looking for the LOR the day after the accident report was filed. LORs were sent to both BI and PIP within 1 day. The provider discovery process was incremental — the client provided some providers on day 1, then additional providers were identified over subsequent calls (TN hospital, TX orthopedic, concussion specialist). This iterative provider discovery extended across weeks. A second accident (occurring one month after the first) was identified during intake, requiring separate claim setup for an NYC cab company (American Transit Insurance).
    42|- In Case F (WC/other case, DOI 12/2022), intake was handled through CaseOpp system. The CaseOpp used the wrong contract type (PI instead of WC), requiring a re-send of WC-specific intake forms. The client's VM was not set up, requiring callback. DocuSign intake docs were viewed multiple times before completion — client reported difficulty with a required field. Despite multiple views, completion took 7 days from first send. Provider identification required a client callback where treatment history was confirmed (ER, orthopedic surgeon, PT).
    43|- In Case G (MVA, DOI 4/2023), intake docs were sent via VineSign on day 1. The welcome call revealed the client had a companion case (daughter) — her file had not been created yet, requiring coordination. Multiple attempts to reach the client failed (VM full), with the firm pivoting to SMS. SSN and health insurance card were not obtained during initial intake and required 3+ follow-up attempts over 2 weeks. The companion case pattern added complexity to intake.
    44|- In Case H (MVA, DOI 12/2022), intake came via a witness/referral (Robert Grant). The firm was unable to reach the client directly — both phone numbers resulted in no answer and no VM. The police report number was obtained from the witness. Eventually the firm reached the client through the witness. This pattern shows that difficult-to-reach clients require alternative contact strategies from the start.
    45|- In Case I (MVA, DOI 1/2024), intake moved from Potential New Client to File Setup to Treatment in a single day. Insurance companies were added, medical providers confirmed via text. The chiro office called proactively about PIP check routing. The client provided provider names via text ("Mary and Elizabeth, Starlite Medical Center"). Rapid same-day intake is possible when the client is responsive.
    46|
    47|## Agent Guidance
    48|
    49|1. Request all necessary information in the first communication (don't spread it across multiple contacts)
    50|2. Send VineSign intake docs within 24 hours of initial contact
    51|3. If docs are not signed within 48 hours, follow up
    52|4. Conduct the welcome call within 24 hours of signed docs
    53|5. Set up insurance claims (LORs to BI and PIP) within 48 hours of signed docs
    54|6. Identify and add all medical providers within the first week
    55|7. Log the phase change from Lead to File Setup once docs are signed
    56|8. For referral cases (husband calling for wife, etc.), get the actual client to sign the docs
    57|
    58|## Pitfalls
    59|
    60|- Slow VineSign turnaround — follow up if not signed within 48 hours
    61|- Playing phone tag with the client for days during intake (use text/email as backup)
    62|- Not capturing all insurance information (both BI and PIP carriers) in the initial contact
    63|- Not asking about all medical providers during the welcome call
    64|- Not sending LORs promptly (this delays adjuster acknowledgment and can delay everything downstream)
    65|
    66|## Related
    67|
    68|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — intake triggers the first phase transitions
    69|- [[client-communication-through-family]] — intake often involves a family member
    70|- [[file-transfer-from-prior-counsel]] — transfer cases have a modified intake workflow
    71|", "total_lines": 70, "file_size": 6312, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|
    17|# Case Phase Transitions
    18|
    19|> PI cases follow a defined lifecycle: Lead -> Potential New Client -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Negotiation -> Settlement. Each transition has specific prerequisites and is triggered by specific events. Understanding these transitions is critical for case management automation.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Observed phase transitions across cases:
    24|
    25|### Lead -> File Setup (or via Potential New Client)
    26|- **Trigger**: Intake docs signed, client info received
    27|- **Prerequisites**: Signed fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, basic client info
    28|- **Actions on transition**: Create welcome call task, send welcome email, set up insurance claims, begin records requests
    29|- **Timeline**: Same day to a few days after initial contact

Now I have a comprehensive understanding of all four cases and the existing wiki. Let me compile my analysis and begin making updates. The key patterns from these 4 cases:

**Case: kimberly-brasher** (premises liability, litigation)
- PL case: slip/fall at Walgreens, multiple defendants (Walgreen Co, Networks USA XIV Inc, REIT Property Management Inc), Sedgwick CMS as adjuster
- Filed in McLean County Circuit Court (24-CI-00012)
- 10+ medical providers across multiple specialties, high medical bills ($137K+)
- UHP Administrators health insurance lien ($35K+)
- Mediation conducted (Thomas J. Knopf Mediation Services)
- Active litigation with defense counsel correspondence through July 2025
- Records still being collected in July 2025 (calling providers for bills/records)

**Case: robyn-hester** (wrongful death, litigation)
- Wrongful death case - decedent pronounced dead at scene
- Representative (Robert Sneed) is the client contact
- Multi-carrier: Progressive BI, Ryder National Liabilities BI, Geico BI
- Filed in Marshall County Circuit Court (23-CI-00073)
- Active discovery, depositions, settlement/release negotiations
- Workers comp liens from Legal Funding Partners ($4K) and Thrivest Link ($171K+)
- Medicare question arose during settlement
- Long investigation/litigation (DOI 2022, still active 2025)

**Case: julmonzhae-moore** (auto accident, litigation)
- MVA with multiple carriers: Liberty Mutual BI (settled), State Farm BI (settled), Elco Insurance BI (open), Liberty Mutual PIP
- Companion case with another plaintiff (filed as Williams, Ward, Moore v. Robinson)
- Filed in Floyd County Indiana Circuit Court
- Deposition scheduling, mediation scheduling
- TeamCare health insurance lien ($3.3K)
- Client calling office frequently for updates (frustration pattern)
- Certified records with billing errors (Starlite Chiropractic)
- EvenUP used for evaluation

**Case: estate-of-keith-graser** (workers comp, litigation)
- WC case: decedent worker (Keith Graser), estate represented by Nancy Graser
- Filed WC claim, DWC proceedings, assigned ALJ Monica Rice-Smith
- BRC hearings, hearings scheduled and rescheduled multiple times over 3+ years
- Defense counsel denied claim initially (no living person named, no supporting docs)
- Motion to substitute plaintiff (estate for individual)
- Multiple IME through Commonwealth IME ($1,750+ in IME costs)
- KY DWC LMS system notifications throughout
- WC Board Opinion received (concurred with ALJ)
- DOI 2020, still active 2025 - 5+ year timeline

## Agent Guidance", "new_string": "  - Case timeline: 3+ years from DOI and still active (Jan 2023 to Mar 2026+)
- In a second WC case (estate claim, DOI 12/2020), the worker died and the estate (represented by the surviving spouse) filed the WC claim. Key observations:
  - The initial claim was denied via DC Form 111 on grounds of "no living person named" and "no supporting documents" — requiring a Motion to Substitute Plaintiff (estate for individual) filed via KY DWC LMS
  - DWC scheduling order assigned an Administrative Law Judge, with BRC hearing and formal hearing dates set
  - Defense counsel filed RFPD (Request for Production of Documents) and contested the claim on multiple grounds
  - Forms 104 and 105 were filed along with 45-Day Disclosures and supporting medical documentation
  - Commonwealth IME was used for medical evaluation (3 separate IME invoices totaling $1,750+), confirming this is the standard WC IME vendor
  - BRC hearings were repeatedly rescheduled over a 3+ year period (DOI 2020, proceedings through 2025)
  - The case resolved through ALJ opinion, which was concurred by the WC Board — demonstrating the full WC administrative resolution pathway
  - LMS (Labor Management System) document notifications were the primary court communication channel, sent via no-reply@ky.gov
  - Total case timeline: 5+ years from DOI (12/2020) through Board opinion (2/2025) — significantly longer than the first observed WC case
  - This case confirms that WC estate claims add procedural complexity (substitution of parties, probate requirements) on top of the already complex WC process

## Agent Guidance"}

This represents a unique litigation event — client death during active WC proceedings requiring immediate procedural action.
+- In a PL case (retail slip-and-fall, McLean County 24-CI-00012), complaint was filed against three corporate defendants (retail chain, property management company, property owner). Defense counsel from a large firm actively engaged in email correspondence about case status. Mediation was conducted but was unsuccessful. The case continued through active litigation with ongoing records collection from 10+ medical providers. Court reporter and mediation expenses were tracked ($400 mediation, $308 court reporting). The multi-defendant PL structure with separate defense representation confirms the pattern that PL cases produce the most litigation-intensive posture.
+- In a wrongful death case (Marshall County 23-CI-00073), the estate representative (surviving family member) was the litigation contact. The case involved three separate BI carriers (two auto carriers + one commercial trucking carrier) and required extensive investigation ($20K+ in investigation costs), accident reconstruction ($2K), and vocational economics evaluation ($3.2K). Court reporter costs across multiple depositions exceeded $1.1K. Settlement release negotiations spanned months with back-and-forth emails. A Medicare question arose during settlement, requiring coordination with defense counsel. This confirms that wrongful death cases in litigation incur substantially higher expert/investigation costs.
+- In an MVA case (Floyd County, IN), complaint was filed as a multi-plaintiff action (companion cases from the same accident). Deposition scheduling involved coordination through court reporting services. The case progressed through discovery to mediation scheduling. Defense counsel correspondence was ongoing through early 2026. The case crossed state lines (KY firm filing in IN court), adding jurisdictional complexity.
+- In a WC estate case (KY DWC claim 202200007), the full WC litigation lifecycle was observed: complaint filing with IROG, initial denial (Motion to Dismiss for "no living person named"), Motion to Substitute Plaintiff, Forms 104/105 and 45-Day Disclosures, BRC hearings (repeatedly rescheduled over 3+ years), defense RFPD, multiple IMEs through Commonwealth IME, ALJ opinion, and WC Board opinion concurring. Total timeline: 5+ years. This confirms the WC administrative proceedings track as fundamentally different from circuit court litigation, with LMS as the filing system.
 - In a multi-defendant auto accident case in litigation (Jefferson Circuit Court, 21-CI-004985, DOI 1/2021), the litigation spanned 4+ years (2021-2025+). Activities included: complaint filing, interrogatory responses to defense counsel, deposition scheduling with defense counsel, State Farm motion to dismiss (AOC 280 Final Submission for Adjudication), certified medical records collection via ChartSwap, government open records requests for police body cam footage (via Axon/Evidence.com), multiple ORRs to LMPD, amended subpoenas (to Verizon and Metro PCS for phone records), Louisville Metro public records requests, forwarding evidence to defense counsel (Dinsmore, Smith & Hoskins), and locating witnesses (other vehicle passengers). This is the most complex litigation case observed, involving multiple defense counsel, government records, and electronic evidence. The 4+ year timeline from DOI to ongoing litigation confirms that multi-defendant cases with uninsured drivers dramatically extend case duration.
 
 ## Agent Guidance
